Welcome to everybody. This is the fourth of fifth calls on the total package teleconference series. I'm Bob Lyon with me. Of course, our Clayton Makepeace as well as Julie McManus. Hi guys. The topic of today's call, which I'll introduce more when we get to it, is the secret that push Clayton Makepeace's income to well over $1 million per year. Well, if I'm doing, first of all, the whole idea of the outline is not to constrict you creatively. But if you use an outline, it does several things for you that are really wonderful. The first thing it does is you don't have to spend a lot of time at the beginning of the job thinking about, oh, what's my major theme? What's my major headline? You know, all of that kind of stuff. And those are the things that cause copywriters to procrastinate a lot at the beginning of the job, which is the absolute worst time to procrastinate. When you have a general idea of the sales that you need to make in the copy, and I'm not talking about just the product sales, as we'll see in a moment, we're talking about you have to make the attention sale, you have to make the credibility sale, you have to make the readership sale, you know, several other sales before you actually can sell the product. And so the outline kind of disciplines, using an outline to begin, disciplines you to make sure that you're making all of those sales in your text. It saves you time because the first step in writing a package that I take is to read the research, to read up on the product, to learn everything I can about the product, to go through the testimonials they've provided. If it's a newsletter, for example, that has a track record for investment, or for help research and breakthroughs or whatever, and there's track record information, I know that's got to be a key part of my package. And so as you're reading through the material that the client sends you about the product, you simply go through, and if you have this outline on an open document, an open Word document on your screen, you're just reading through and you're going, oh, well that fits in the testimonial section, and you just go, you copy it and you paste it over into the testimonial section. Oh, that goes in the track record section, and you do that. So with this, with this outline, you don't have to spend days thinking about what you're going to write. You never start with a blank page. It's beautiful just for that reason. I also use this, and I say it's filling in a template. Yes, absolutely. And I find, for example, that an outline or a template for a newsletter promotional will be different from an outline for a product sale promotion where I'm selling a supplement or some three-dimensional product. But they still need, no matter what you're selling, you still need to accomplish the things in the outline that we sent out the other day. So it's a real great way to get started. You never find yourself sitting there staring at a blank page and saying, holy cow, what am I going to do? And so by the time you're actually ready to start reading, you'll have a document that's 20, 30, 40 pages long that will have in a proper order, an order that makes sense. It doesn't necessarily have to be the final order, but in an order that makes sense, you'll have everything that you need. It's like being a car mechanic and having every tool you're going to need for the job right there at your fingertips. And I strongly suggest in terms of just your writing setup that every copywriter have at least two monitors connected to your computer. I have three. I have a large 32-inch display in the center, and then I have a vertical 24-inch display to my left, which allows me to look at tabloid-sized PDFs at larger-than-life size. So when I'm checking graphics, it's very easy for me to see the entire page and to spot things that I want to change. And then to my right, I have a horizontally-oriented 24-inch. But even just having two displays is great because that way you can have your outline document open on a left-hand display and then the larger research document that's been provided to you by the client or that you've put together yourself or however you receive it, the source documents open on your main screen. So you're doing your reading and then just highlighting or copying text from the one end to the other, and things move very quickly that way. Now, I got an assignment the other day to write a quarter-page space ad. So it looks like the outline of the ones you provided, I'd apply your short copy outline. Can you take us through that? Sure, I can. In short copy, you are strictly limited by the amount of space that you have or the amount of time if it's television. And so you're reduced to just the very fundamental basic elements that are required to make the sale. The first is a headline. I'm still amazed that I see smaller ads that don't have headlines. On television a lot of times, I'll see direct response ads that don't have a headline. They don't grab the prospect's attention with a major benefit or by connecting with how they feel about either the benefit or the lack of that benefit in their lives. And as we've discussed on these calls and in the total package and elsewhere, a headline we find is like 60% of the lift that you could possibly get in subsequent tests of copy. And so the headline is really a major key. Are you there, Bob? Yeah, I'm listening. Okay, good. It got a little quiet. So anyway, the first thing is the headline. And we're going to spend more time on each of these things next week as we do the bonus session, which is actually on the nuts and bolts of copywriting. And I'll be sharing some of the secrets that I've used. Just to remind you guys, listening to the call next week, Wednesday at noon is our fifth and final call. And we're going to ask you to leave an hour and a half in your schedule for it. We originally planned an hour. We want to leave an hour and a half just in case a lot of questions come in. And that way we'll be able to get through all of it. But Clayton is going to go into the actual writing of these elements, headlines and sidebars and subheads that he's presenting in brief in the outline now. Right. So the headline is Anyone Who's Studied Copy. There are many different ways to write it, the right headlines. Again, we'll get into that next time. But there are two elements that tend to be present in just about every headline. I've often said that the only purpose the headline serves is to grab attention. That's all it ever has to do. And I think one of the major mistakes copywriters make, and quite often it tends to be the younger copywriters, is they try to get their entire cell in the headline. And all you really need to do is get the prospect's attention. I remember one of my favorite magazine covers of all time was a National Lampoon Cover with a picture of this poor pathetic looking dog on the cover. And someone was holding a gun to the dog's head. And the headline was By This Magazine or We Shoot the Dog. Well, I mean, they had to sell millions of magazines. I mean, that had to be the best headline ever. There's the old joke about the headline that just has the word sex in a 120 point type. And then the text says, now that we have your attention, what gets people's attention more than anything? Maybe a gunshot behind them. A loud noise, a loud bang. That's all a headline really needs to be at its base, at its essence. Now, that isn't to say that headlines that go farther than that are bad. It's just that, you know, if that's all you have, then fine. You know, it can work. The headline's job is just to get the attention of the person that you're trying to call out of the audience. And again, we'll go through all of the different headline styles that we use next time. But if we're focusing now on the short copy outline, unlike a tabloid where you can then warm it up, you only have like three by five inches of space on that side of the postcard, what do you do after the headline? Now I've got their attention. What do I do? Okay. Well, again, with the headline, even in short copy, there are two other ways to go. Okay, you know, there's the gunshot, the bang, you know, hey, look at this. Tim Rutz once wrote a headline for a health package. It just said, read this or die. You know? I don't remember that. And if there was no benefit, there was no, you know, there was no explicit emotion with that headline. It was just, look, either read this or start making out your will because you're toast, you know? And it worked quite well. The other element, of course, is the benefit and copy gurus, copy teachers go on endlessly about, you know, how you should include benefits in your headline. And when you're doing a short copy, you tend to be going straight for the product sell. You're not, it's not an advertorial. You're not romancing them with value added information or topical stuff. You're going straight to the heart of the matter. So a benefit hit works very well in short copies. And then on television, when you see ads for kitchen gadgets and things like that, you'll notice it quite often or exercise equipment. You'll notice it quite often that you use a dominant emotion headline where they'll basically show a woman trying to flip a pancake. And this poor woman should never be allowed near a stove because, you know, she's making such a mess of it. You're worried, you know, that she might be threatening her own safety. But that connects with the frustration of having utensils that don't work right or that make every job hard. So those headlines are not benefit oriented at all. That is a headline. When you see that woman sitting there trying to flip an egg or a pancake and she can't do it because it keeps lighting up the spatula. That's a headline. They're basically saying, man, isn't this frustrating? Isn't this horrible? And then immediately all the women and guys who like to cook in the audience are going, yeah, that's a real pain. So that's a dominant emotion headline. So even in short copy, you can go for any one of those three. And then from there, then it's time to expand on the headline. And I tend to do it just with a way that explains my headlines because quite often my headlines are kind of by this we shoot the dog headline. The one that just grabs people's attention, especially in short copy. So then expanding on the headline. Now, Ben Sevaga and a lot of the other people who have written books on writing headlines like the Ogldies of the world and so forth make a big deal out of the fact that the headline should include a proposition of some kind. If this, then that, or give me 30 days and I'll do that, or whatever, some kind of a proposition. And propositions can be more subtle than that too. They really, and this is some place that we really need to get more thought to and we'll be talking about next time. But a proposition headline often contains a very strong intrigue element. So, or a statement that immediately gets the prospects hypnotic. But anyway, the very next thing he reads has to connect to the headline. A lot of copywriters I see, they'll start out with a headline that's going to the one direction, it's going to the left, and then the copy that immediately follows it is a total disconnect. And that's just a horrible mistake to make because immediately the prospects confuse and lose his interest. So that deck copy needs to expand on the concept or the theme that was established in the headline. And then the next thing is, at this point, if a person is reading the ad, there's only one question in his mind. We all do this because we're all seasoned consumers and we've all seen pitches of all kinds that just weren't credible to us. So we all tend to be skeptics when we come to advertising. And so the next thing that you need to do is establish the credibility of what you're saying. And in short copy, because you're going straight to a product sale, the credibility element probably will have to do directly with the product. And it could be either a testimonial or it could be a statement about the credentials of the person who's speaking this to me to be making this claim. Or it could be a statement of fact about the product. 300,000 happy users swear that this really does do whatever it's supposed to do. It could be a statement by a recognized authority. Just anything to bring credibility to this message. Carleen always asks the question, so what? Well, I always ask the question, why should I believe you? You're telling me this thing is going to solve a problem in my life. It's either going to remove a fear, ease of frustration, or give me something that my heart desires. And that interests me. So now you've got to show me why I should listen to you and why I should believe that your message to me is credible. So what? It digs deeper for benefits. Why should I believe you dig deeper to credibility? That's correct. Absolutely. And then the next thing that you do then, now you've got your attention, you've expanded on it, you've added credibility. Yes, I can really do this for you. And then now you dig into the benefits more deeply. And that's where you repeat the headline benefit with either substantiation or additional details that make it very believable. And then add any other benefits that the product has that you can fit in the space allotted. And this is a very important section. I also make sure that the infomercial people and television people are excellent at this because television is perceived from the outset to be a far more emotional medium than direct mail or Internet because we have moving pictures. We see people responding and reacting to situations and we can live vicariously that event or that situation through them. So people who do television are very good at connecting with the resident emotion of being overweight and embarrassed about it. Or on the other side of it, there's a great Bowflex commercial with this really hot looking 50-something year old woman and she's got this 20-year-old something boyfriend and they're hanging out by the pool and she's in her bikini and she looks just spectacular. And talking about my sister, hey, watch it. Oh, is that? I think it's obvious I like that ad. But it's wonderful because every woman watching it is looking at it and going, wow, she really does look better at 50 than a lot of people do in their 20s. And if Bowflex can do that, that's what I want. So they're connecting with a positive resident emotion, the desire there that people have for a better body. And I think I bet you're more guised by as a result of that ad than women because it's just a fun ad to watch. But this is where you get into the benefits again and try very hard to connect each one of those benefits with an emotion and show them how that is going to, that benefit is going to either erase a fear, ease of frustration, or fulfill an emotional desire. And then the next step is also something that is quite often overlooked and that is, you know, you need to tell people exactly what you want them to do. Okay, ordering is easy. First do this then do that. Maybe easy two-step ordering or whatever. Now in print, you only have to tell them once. On television, you'll notice that they repeat the toll-free number. You know, you're assuming that the person who's hearing the ad is doing their ironing or going, you know, reading their mail or also sitting there with a magazine or having a conversation with friends. And they may want the product but they don't have a pencil right there to write down the 800 number and they're not next to their phone. And so the number appears on the screen through the entire ad at the bottom of the screen. And then also the number is repeated anywhere from three to five times and in some cases even more often, especially in like 60 or 90-second spots. So that it gives people time to hear the number and to find their pen and write it down. So, you know, those are the basic elements of short copy ads. So that'll work like for a one-minute or a 90-second TV commercial, it'll work for a small print ad. What about now if we switch now to the medium copy outline, do we take a completely different approach or do we just take the same approaches in the short copy and add elements? No, you know, selling is selling. If you were selling to somebody face to face, the first thing you'd have to do is get them to look at you and listen to you. That's the attention sale, right? And the second thing that you'd have to do is you would present a benefit or show them how you're going to help them. And then you would just move down through this process. You would have to make the same number of sales and it doesn't matter what medium you're in or how long or short the copy is, you still need to make the same sales. With medium length copy, you have more room to fully develop these things and you have more time to add other elements that will help you make these essential sales. And so once again, you know, medium copy, if you're following along in the PDF you received, you'll see this. The first thing again is the headline. Again, support that headline. And then in longer copy, you're requiring a time commitment from your prospect. And so I think it's very important and I do this on every cover of every long copy self-mailer I do. I bribe the prospect to read this and this is where the advertorial approach comes in. I tell him that I'm going to give him some information for free in this report before you finish reading this. You will have learned. Okay. And I'll give him two or three or five or sometimes seven. On a boardroom piece, it could be, you know, 12 or 13 or 14. Things that are going to be revealed inside. And on supplement promotions, especially when there's a, as part of the offer, there's a free supplement that they receive, you know, buy three, get one free or something. I'll also say free supplements, you know, free vitamin D, free, you know, whatever. And then put a value on what I'm going to, you know, that there's a certificate in close that will get them this thing free. So now, wow, they're really bribe to read this thing. It's hard to turn down something that's free. Somebody said that the best ad ever written was just a two word ad that said free beer. And I think that's true. I mean, you know, you're going to get a hundred percent response rate on something like that. So offer them something free inside, whether it's information that's going to bring value to their life or actually something such as a certificate or some other monetized element inside that actually gives them a dollar value. Can that be something as simple as special reports? Yes, yeah. The one thing on special reports, and I run up against this all the time, for the people who are listening who are writing newsletter promotions, there's this little law that the U.S. Post Office enforces that says that if the newsletter is delivered by a second class male, and many are, that the offer used to sell the newsletter, the free gifts that are offered cannot have a value higher than what you collected for the subscription. So if it's a $39 subscription that you're selling, and I mean, okay, on pricing and newsletters, there's always a nominal price, an actual price. A nominal price is, say, $78, and what you're charging for it is a half-priced discount offer, and you're charging half of that, $39. The total value of the premiums the free gifts you're offering cannot be greater than the $39. So when you see newsletter promotions that don't, you know, they always offer premiums free gifts. When you see promotions where the free gifts don't have a versus as a $29 value free, and then at the end on the response device, they don't add it all up and say, you get $1,000 with the free gifts, you know, for buying a subscription. That's because they're delivering the newsletter via second class male. I'm a big advocate for delivering newsletters via first class male simply because it allows you to advertise the value of the premiums that you're giving away free. And by doing that, you know, now all of a sudden, for example, especially in financial things where the newsletter is more expensive, a $99 newsletter, $169 for two years, you're able to say, okay, yeah, it's $99, and that's a screaming deal because not only is it a half-priced offer, but you also get $500 worth of free gifts, you know, free reports. And I never call them reports. It's always volumes or books or, you know, reports sound kind of cheap, you know. But this is a volume, or I combine the reports into a library, you know, the better investing library or whatever, and then give it a real value. There's some legal things to that too that the report should have actually been advertised on the Internet at that price at whatever value you attach to it. Just so if FTC ever comes back to you or anyone comes back to you, you can say, no, really, this was advertised nationally at that price. Whether anyone bought them at that price or not is immaterial, but so long as they were advertised at price nationally, then you can say that it's a $79 value or whatever it is. So anyway, all that just to say that the, we're bribing him to read this, okay? And the, you know, just kind of keep in mind too that quite often, you know, the value of information is much greater than the number of pages or the perceived value of the document. Everyone knows a book, hardcover book is only worth $29. But a special report that's going to erase my debt in 30 days or less is that information is worth thousands of dollars to me. So it's the perceived value of the information, not the format that it's delivered in. So demeaning that information by calling it a report or a book just kind of lowers the perceived value. Okay, so brought him to read it, then what? You've done that on the cover. Yes, and I also tend to like to use my covers to actually start the conversation. You'll notice when you see a lot of megalogs out there, though, the front looks just like a magazine cover. And so there's only teaser copy for what's inside. And that's effective, it works fine. But you want to create momentum as people go through a project and through the copy. And the best way I've found to do that is to go with more of a report style format that doesn't have a dedicated cover. It just simply has the headline, the debt copy, why they should read this, which is essentially a short table of contents, you know, that's on the cover, and then dives right into the sales copy. So the next thing that I do is I've established momentum with my headline and debt copy and told them what I wanted, what I'm going to do for them. Then the next thing I think is very important is to reconfirm all of that in my opening sentences of copy, where I help the reader see himself living this better life with the benefits that I've just promised him, or with the benefits of reading this piece. Do you want to position it that you help himself seeing the better life, the benefits, or do you want to focus on the problem that your product is going to solve? I can do it both ways, but either way I come back to the benefits. Let's say for example, if the first paragraph, in the first paragraph I can do one or two things, and Bob, I know why you're asking this question, because Bob is working on a package right now where we kind of brainstormed it together, and do you begin with dimensionalizing the problem that the prospect has, or do you begin by helping him see himself living this better life? I think it just depends on the product. Sometimes, let's say for example, it's a vision product, and you're talking to older people who know their vision is failing, and they're frustrated with wearing multiple pairs of glasses. I think a great headline for a sidebar would be, hey, six eyes, and just show a guy wearing three pair of glasses to read something. I've been caught doing that before when I have my contacts out, and I can't find the right pair of reading glasses. I'll just stack them up until I can finally see the page. You could start out with the embarrassment and frustration of going into a restaurant and you can't read the menu. You were trying to dial a phone number, and you can't read your notes, and you can't find your glasses. You're not the fear of driving at night when your vision is fading, and really kind of connect with the guy and say, I know how you feel, but imagine how it would be if instead of getting a stronger prescription every year, you doctor told you that your eyes were actually improving, and then paint the positive picture right away. The negative by itself, all that does is get the guy's head nodding and saying, oh, this guy knows how I feel, and that helps. But then at other times, but then you need to move quickly to the benefit. Yeah, Arthur Johnson says that in the health package, you need to give them hope. Yes, that's right. But then sometimes the negative is so obvious that the laboring is just going to lose it. So if I'm working on the negative, it really helps you connect with this person, then wonderful. But on the other hand, if the laboring, the negative scares him, depresses him, discourages him from reading, you've really not done yourself a favor. And so in those cases, it may be better to go straight into the benefits. I'm in Washington, D.C. right now, and I was visiting with a client the other day, and they actually brought in a package that I wrote in 1991 that was responsible for the founding of a company that now sells hundreds of millions of dollars worth of supplements every year. And they gave me their only extra remaining copy. I think they have one framed in the building, but they had one more in their files, and they gave it to me because I lost mine many years ago. And on that package, I started out just by saying, give me 90 days, and I'll help you. And then it was just all the positive stuff that I'm going to do. So that's fine too. And you just kind of fill your way through that. And then you go on to the credibility elements next. And again, that getting as juice is flowing, you want to create momentum. So we're only talking about two or three, maybe four quick paragraphs. Then I go into, hi, I'm Dr. So-and-so, or hi, I'm whoever, and here are my credentials. And I've helped other people do this, or here's my track record, or here's what five people have said about what I've done for them. And you weave in credibility right up front again there. And this is longer than what you can do in a deck. It's longer than what you could do with that cover copy. But again, you're just, you're returning to what you told them you would tell them. I quite often tell young writers, just follow this format and you'll be fine. Tell them what you're going to tell them. Tell them, and then tell them what you told them. So that really helps the reader. Oh, this is what we're going to talk about next. Okay, we're talking about it. Okay, now that we're done talking about it, that's what we talked about. And draw your conclusions and drive it home. That's just a nice little formula to use when writing sidebars or really writing anything. So credibility is next. Now, and then the next thing is to dive into the guts of the package. Right. At that point, the copy is, well, I told you I was going to tell you how to reverse heart disease. Okay, here's how you reverse heart disease. And you put in that section. And then quite often then you can, if that's as far as your product goes, then fine, just fully develop the heart disease angle or whatever the benefit is. But a lot of times you'll be using that headline concept as a springboard and to prove a larger point, which is that, for example, alternative remedies work better than drugs or that this particular investment approach works better than what everyone else tells you to do. So in cases where it's a springboard, you might then move from having fulfilled on your headline to, oh, and by the way, this approach also works great for arthritis and diabetis and this and that and just start spreading a broader and broader net. Got it. Dan, where do you go from there? Well, and it's time finally to make your offer. As you're moving through, by the way, if it's a newsletter package, when you're presenting your fulfilling on your headline for heart, there's usually a free gift that you'll tell them about in a moment, but in that gift I'm going to give you all the details of my complete program or whatever. And then if you move on, if it's a springboard type of situation, then you move on to arthritis and that section would culminate in an offer for a premium. And so by the time they get to the offer point in the package, in the copy, it's time to add it all up and for the first time now, they're going to see all seven or eight or 12 premium titles together. And if you're able to give value to the premiums, again, if the guy's not mailing second class, delivering his product second class, then it's time to add up the value of everything that they get. So now you have a total premium value and he's going, wow, how much, you know, your prospect is thinking, wow, that's a lot of cool stuff. I bet it's going to cost me a fortune. And so then it's time to start talking about how to get all of this free. And I forget who it was Bob, maybe you remember, they used to run full page ads in New York Times and all the major papers selling books. And they would never sell the book, they would write the entire ad about the premium, and then about two-thirds of the way through the copy, they would have just a very simple headline would say, how to get this wonderful volume. I don't remember that. I was saying who it was. How to get this volume free, you know? And then it would say, well, you know, I'd love to send this to everybody. It would save hundreds of lives a year or it would make, you know, create an extra 500 millionaires this year. But obviously, you know, I can't afford to send this to every home in the country. But my publisher and I have figured out a way to get it to you free. And the way to get it free is to buy this other wonderful book that's only $29 or whatever the price is. So it's important to justify why you want to send them all this stuff. And it's never, you know, you don't want it to come off feeling like, feeling like you're just trying to sell them something. You want to send them these free gifts because they're really going to help them. And it's urgent that they get them quickly because they can do these wonderful things for them. And you've figured out a way to get them to them with absolutely no cost. And you can get them by trying or accepting or applying for a free trial or a risk-free trial of the product itself, the main product. And you introduce the product at that point and you still haven't mentioned price. And you introduce the product at that point and you sell the heck out of it, all the benefits, all the wonderful things this product is going to do in my life. And then now the prospect is thinking, this is fantastic. I bet this is going to cost a fortune. Now it's time to present your price. And when you present your price to dimensionalize, that it's the best bargain there ever was anywhere. Just show it. I mentioned this before. I like comparing my, what people pay for my products to what they're paying right now for some inconsequential item. And somehow Starbucks keeps coming up because people will pay four or five dollars once or twice a day for cups of Starbucks coffee and not even give it a second thought. Well, if you're paying eight dollars a day for Starbucks, that's 240 dollars a month, right? That's what, almost three thousand dollars a year you're paying for Starbucks? Yep. You know, what's it doing besides tasting good and giving you the jitters? Is it curing your heart disease? Is it making you rich? Is it locking in a secure retirement? Is it giving you a flashy new car? Is it making you more attractive? I just think it's mainly making you go to the bathroom more. Yeah. Expensive urine, right? And so it's, when you compare the value on a daily basis, that's, you know, the technique there of course is you take, look, this newsletter is $99 a year. It's going to do all these wonderful things for you. That's only, you know, whatever it is, 30 cents a day or whatever that breaks down to. Less than the price of a first class postage stamp. Yeah, exactly. And what does the postage stamp get you? You know, your bills paid, I guess, but you know, that's it. So make the value of the product just amazing. Well, I do. Go ahead. No, no, go ahead. Now, I do want to make sure we do get to the main feature today of, you know, your new formula for copywriting and how you got to the million dollar mark. So what else should we cover about the closing of our package for our medium copy outline? On the closing, just, you're right, we're running late here. On the closing, I think it's very important to repeat your main benefits. A lot of people will forget to do this, but you got their attention with your wonderful headline and deck copy and with your opening benefit-oriented copy, right? Telling them all the things you're going to do for them. In my clothes, I like to say, wow, we've only been together a few minutes today and look at all the things that I've already been able to do for you. You know, I showed you this, I taught you that. I gave you three ways to do this. I gave you six ways to do that. And then not only that, but I offered SIN to give you completely free, without obligation, X dollars worth of these wonderful free gifts. And then I showed you how that, you know, in addition to that, you can save half or whatever the amount is off on the main product. And I've showed you that you've got to be delighted or you get all your money back and you can keep everything I sent you. And you just basically are trying to demonstrate how this is a total no-brainer. You'd have to be a drooling moron, not to say yes, not to speed down me now and buy the product. And so that's the close. And in the PS, I like to surprise them with an additional premium for fast response. Quick response bonus. Yes, fast response bonus. And next time we'll talk about the different ways to do that. You can reward them for going to the net and ordering. There's some things you need to think about there before you decide to do that. You can reward them for calling in. 80% of your orders will come over the phone anyway, not through the order form. So you want to make this kind of an impulse for them. You want them to do it now and not think about it or put it off and to do it later and then forget about it. You want them to move now. So maybe it could be reward them for ordering by phone. Or most people what they do is just say, look, this is an early birth bonus. You order, no matter how you order, you just order within the next 10 days and you get it free. So I use a PS for that usually because they think you've just, they've seen your entire offer. Sort of the version of the infomercial where the old Alvin Icoff, but wait, there's more. Exactly. But I do want to get to the main topic of conference for today, which we're calling the secret that pushed Quayton make these income to well over one million per year. And the whole, I think this session, section, is going to be fascinating to new copywriters. To people who've been around, and I'll use myself as an example, some of you guys might find it a little upsetting because it basically says that the standard way that the copywriting business is run, the way we've been doing it, in some cases for 5, 10, or in my case 25 years, yikes, is not the best way to do it, is wrong. And you call that the way that most people do it, you call it the freelance copywriter paradigm. It's the business model where the way freelance copywriting operates for 99.9% of people, which is basically you go find a client, you get an assignment, you negotiate a fee, you learn the product, you interview the client, you write the copy, you change it, and you build a client and that's it. And you say that this costs the copywriters and the client time and money, and it's not the best way to do it. Why is that? Well, think about the things that go into being a freelancer. And we've talked about how important time is, if you're in any kind of business, and especially copywriting, you only have a certain number of hours per day and per year that you can be actually productive as a copywriter. And during that time, you don't get paid for anything but writing great copy, you don't get paid for the invoicing or any of the other stuff, you don't get paid for research, you don't get paid for going through the learning curve that you have to go through on every new product. You don't get paid for establishing the relationship between yourself and the marketing manager or the copy chief. You don't get paid for that time. That's stuff you have to do for free. And so every month you wake up and you say, oh well, okay, it's time to start my next project and you go to work and you go through 12 learning curves every year if you write 12 packages a year, where you have to learn about what the client likes, what he doesn't like, what's worked in the past, what hasn't worked, what the product is. If it's a newsletter or an informational product of some kind, you have to learn about the editor or the author and what his background was. You have to learn about the best ways to present the product itself. And that's all learning curve time. And it can take weeks on every job. It can take up to half of the time that you spend on a job for a client. And then you finally get all of the work done and the client accepts the copy and you move on to the next product that's for another client usually. And the first one might be for Phil's publishing, next month might be a Cora, or whoever it is you're working with in my case, it would be like that. And you have to go through that learning curve all over again. At the same time, you're calling the client up and the client wants you to be successful, but the client has to be demure about actual test results that they've had in the past. Your basic copywriter contract with them and the relationship that you have with them is a very loose knit one. And if you ask them, well, have you tested this against that? This type of offer, for example, against that kind of offer, this kind of headline against that kind of headline, they'll say, yeah, we have. You'll say, well, what did you learn? Well, they'll say this offer worked better than that offer. But then if you press them farther, you'll say, well, okay, was the response rate higher on this one, or was it the average unit of sale? Or how strong was the copy or the package? How large was the test? How statistically significant are the numbers? How long ago did you test it? I know people have tested one thing 15 years ago on one package. And they consider that the commandment of their lives. Yeah, and now it's company doctrine. You start pressing like that and they get more and more demure until finally they say, well, we can't really release that information. Ask them what list they tested it on. Were these soft offer buyers or hard offer buyers? So at some point they have instructions from their superiors that certain information is kind of like the crown jewels, and they're not going to be sharing that information with you. So you have kind of an arms length relationship with the guy, because he's not going to share stuff with you that could really help you write a better package and get bigger winners more off and bigger royalties more off. So the model has worked reasonably well for a couple of decades. But I found out back in the 80s that pursuing a different kind of relationship with clients opens up enormous opportunities both for the client and the copywriter. And the whole thing came to me really with security rare coin. I had written the package for security that sold 120,000 subscribers to their financial newsletter. And I told the story on the series of calls how I challenged the publisher to allow me to write a back end promotion for him and how that promotion increased his sales of his product, which happened to be rare coins, by tenfold in the first 30 days. And by the end of the year we were selling 44 times more coins than he had been, 44 times more dollar value of coins than he had been selling previously. The first part of that was, as I explained in another call, the first part of that was going with the dominant emotion of a coin buyer. That got us our tenfold list instead of talking about this coin is made out of silver and this got this much silver and it would probably go up this much in value. I talked about how it was a time machine and it was in White-Earth's pocket. It had gone by the okay corral and so forth. By adding the dominant emotion to it we got that tenfold lift. But over the next 12 months we got a 44-fold lift because as soon as I did that for the guy, he said, look, I want a closer relationship with you. And he said, obviously you can take my company to the moon. And so I want us to really work in harness and I want you to be exclusive with me. And that way you'll have a confidentiality agreement, you'll have an exclusivity agreement with me, and I'll pay you $250,000 a year as a retainer. What year was this? 1982. I'll pay you $250,000 a year retainer. I'll give you a Cadillac. I'll pay you $40,000 plus moving expenses just to move closer to my office so that you're more available. I will give you 10% of net sales and I will give you monthly bonuses based on how well your house file promotions work. And I will give you the standard royalty you get on the outside promotions. And in 1982, all of that added up to, you know, I don't remember the exact number but as you can quite imagine it was huge. And because we were selling $16 million a month in rare coins by the end of the year. And so it was that relationship that made me realize that the freelance copywriter model is, although it's worked okay, it leaves a lot of money on the table for the clients and for the copywriters. Just yesterday, I was with a major mailer here in Washington, D.C. area and we were here to speak expressly about building copywriter cells for this publisher. As I've said before, the one thing that presents financial publishers from growing, from adding new products and really growing their existing products is copy. It's the one missing ingredient. They can find all kinds of doctors who agree to write books and edit health newsletters. There are tens of thousands of them that would love to do it. They can find all kinds of financial advisors who would like to do that. They can find all kinds of people who would love to come in and go to work for them and run their marketing departments and do those kinds of things. The one thing that limits the size of their company is the fact that they can't get enough good copy to do those, to bring on the new products and to grow the products that they have. It's made worse, as copy shortage is made worse by the fact that the Internet has come along. With the Internet, they've got this whole new marketing channel. All of these new kinds of copy that they have to do, short email blasts, longer landing pages, all of this other kind of copy that can't be directly stolen or adapted from things they're writing for other things that a lot of it needs to be created out of whole cloth. People talk about a gas shortage. There is an enormous copy shortage in this country. This company had me in to explore ways that I could introduce new copywriters to them, copy chief them through, and then at the end of the year hand them a sell of two or three new copywriters that are exclusive to them that can take their company to the moon. That requires, I made it very clear up front, what that requires is that you be totally open with me about your test results. I will sign something that says that if I ever say anything about your confidential information to any other company that you end up owning my house, my car, my kids, everything else, because I'm not going to do that. They don't get the redhead too, do they? Yeah. Say water. But she's giving me a dirty look right now. She should. But anyway, so we established a very close relationship now. Their books are open to me. I can ask them, I want data cards on your 20 best performing mailing lists. That's information they would never dream of sharing with a copywriter, but because we have this contract, this new relationship that's a very deep one, they're able to tell me that. Now I can look at those data cards and I can see exactly what their prospects are buying, how much they're paying for it. Not only that, I can get ahold of the promotion pieces that their prospects responded to. So as I'm creating sales promotions to talk to those people, I know what they've already bought. I know the selling points that they responded well to. So I've gone much deeper than just finding out what my client has learned in the mail. I'm finding out what the list owners that he's mailing to have learned in the mail. So it's equipped me to really do a much better job for them. Now I'm going to put you on the hot seat. People listening are thinking, this sounds good, but we can see why they offer this to you because you are who you are and have this fabulous track record. And there are some guys listening to this call, David, you're listening, and a couple others who are in a similar position to be asked to do this stuff. But there may be other people listening to the call and say, no one's approaching me like this. How do I even get to the position where I can make this switch? Can it be done only with the big direct marketers like the company you're talking about or can you do this with any company? Can you do it with a local company that makes boilers or water purifiers? Well, I think the answer to that is yes. And I'll tell you why. Well, first of all, let me tell you how I approach it. No, let me tell you about Boilermakers. Okay. Yeah, every company basically wants to grow. That's their goal in life. That's why everyone puts on their monkey suits in the morning on Monday and goes to work. They're going to work to make the company grow. The one thing that they need to make the company grow is new customers and to sell more things to existing customers. The thing that makes that happen in the companies that we deal with is copy. And so establishing a relationship with any company that's an ongoing relationship. Okay. In the advertising world, they've been doing this forever. You know, if you want McCann-Erikson to do something for you, you don't just call him up and say, hey, I need a direct mail piece. What you do is you go to McCann-Erikson and then you say, hi guys, we're interviewing agencies. We're considering hiring an agency. And then you call BBD and O and all of the other guys. And if they come and they make their pitch to you, if the account is of sufficient size that it interests them, the result then is a contract that says over the next year, you are going to pay them so much in retainers, plus you're going to pay them on their standard fee schedule for television spots and print ads and for all of the other things that they do for you. So that's how the advertising industry has worked forever. It's only been in our industry that if you're a copywriter, you're either a freelancer or you were an in-house guy, or you were a major direct response agency that had your own in-house writers. In many cases, though, the major direct response agencies are not that great because what happens with copywriters? If you've got an in-house copywriter who's doing well and making a lot of money for the agency's clients, the minute that his name is out there, he's a goner because you can't pay him enough to keep the amount of staff. If he can make a million dollars a year as a freelancer, why in the world would he want to keep taking his six-figure salary from the agency? Because we're a proof-based industry, whereas advertising tends more to be if you have a clever ad and the client likes it, you've got the client's happy and everything's cool. But with us, it comes down to counting the numbers at the end of the day. And we could and should at some point. There may be some advertising people on the call whose heads are about to explode after hearing that, but the fact of the matter is with advertising, if you are advertising Coca-Cola and you launch a new campaign this month and sales go down, it could be because Pepsi had a strong campaign, it could be because it was cooler weather and not as many people were thirsty, it could be any number of things, not attributable to your ad, whereas in direct response, you know it is attributable. So anyway, this model's been used for years in the advertising world. Going to a company that wants to grow and that recognizes that they need sales copy to do that, and then establishing a relationship. I don't care what they're selling, earth shoes, anything. Isn't that difficult to do? The way that I do it nowadays, after having done that at SRC, a security recline, then after having done it with Blanchard and Company, both companies, SRC went up 4,400% in sales, Blanchard quadrupled. Then I went on to Phillips publishing where we launched Health and Healing and other things and wound up with a very close relationship with them that included a monthly retainer, plus royalties, plus, plus, plus, that included consulting as well as copywriting. And then I went from there to Weiss and did it, we quadrupled that company. At Phillips, we sold over 2 million subscriptions, then after that, and did, I moved down to Weiss and we quadrupled Weiss's customer file and their revenues. Each time it worked, each time confidential information was exchanged that wouldn't be exchanged with a copywriter, each time the learning curve was gone through once for each product, for each franchise in the company, each time I was able to buy coffee. Are you there? Yes. Okay. Each time I was able to recycle coffee, which saved me a lot of time by recycling, I mean the back end copy was, hang on just a second, each time the back end copy in a promotion could stand because it was mine. So I could use it, reuse it over and over again and just focus on the front end where most of the response happened. So there are a lot of efficiencies of scale that went along with it. And so, you know, in looking back over my career and realizing on the first one, I just kind of stumbled into it by dumb luck. And the second one, Blanchard, I had a reputation for growing their competitor who was a nobody into the biggest company in the industry. So it was only natural that I move on to another coin company. Then when that company was sold, the freelancing with Phillips rolled into this deeper relationship. But at that point, did you say to Phillips, look, this is what I did with my coin clients, you should do this with me or did they also approach you and say, you know, can we hire you on an exclusive basis? No, no. At that point, I knew that I wanted a deeper relationship with Phillips. And of course, they wanted to take me off the market because the strength of the copy I had done for Health and Healing, it was a brand new industry that we were creating and they didn't want me creating competing products. And so it was in their interest to take me off the market. I knew that the closer relationship would yield dividends that they didn't know that because they had never tried that before. And of course, it did. I ended up launching all of their new products for many years. In fact, when we were talking to them the other day, they trotted out, they were giving us history on some things that they had history for some of their products. And these are mostly new people that had no idea who I was or what my history with the company was and they were bringing in all of these old direct mail packages from 15 years ago and saying, well, this is how it launched and this package was great and they mailed all these pieces. And I had written every one of them. But anyway, after having kind of stumbled into this kind of situation many times, at Phillips I just said, you know, I know what's going to happen here. This is just going to deepen the relationship. It will be wonderful. So I went for it. At Weiss though, I went to Martin and I got to be kind of good buddies because I had written several packages for him and I said, hey, you know, why don't we go on a cruise together, and we'll issue and meet Elizabeth and we'll jump on a cruise ship and go down to the Caribbean for a week and it'd just be a nice getaway and maybe we'll do some general brainstorming or whatever. And it was just kind of a fun thing. I remember we were walking up the stairs one morning. Women were still in bed and Martin and I were walking up the staircase to get up on deck and he said, you know, we've done so much good in the last six months, you know, in terms of launching new products and getting some strong controls. I just want to keep that going. And I said, well, you know, if you want to, I know how to really take this company to the moon. I said, you just want to do it. And he goes, what do you have in mind? And I said, well, you know, you've seen what I can do. We work well together. We're good buddies. Why don't you just go ahead and why don't we just get married? You know, let's go ahead and make this a formal thing where we're basically partners in this thing. And I'm a part of your marketing team. I will build a marketing team for you. I will, you know, do all of these, you know, I will bring in copywriters. I'll copy chief them. I'll be exclusive with you on the front end. You won't have to worry about my availability. I'll be there for you. If I need other copywriters, I'll bring them in. You'll have plenty of copy. And he jumped at it. And so we went straight back after the cruise and drew up a contract, got married. And that relationship lasted from 1999 through 2004. Now, a couple of questions for you. When you do that kind of relationship, you, let's say with Weiss, you essentially, did you essentially or totally take yourself off the market? Were you unavailable to everybody else? Yeah, there had to be a transition period. Right. And essentially what I do now and what I did with Weiss. Now I'm much more calculating in how I do it. You know, I approached, instead of with Weiss, I'll talk about another client that I'm working with now. I went to the client. The client actually came to us and wanted us to write some little assignment. Right. And I just said, I'm not interested in that. I am not interested in doing freelance. You know, I can make decent money doing that, but it's a pain. And I don't feel like I can give you my very best that way. And I said, but I will accept a freelance assignment from you on these conditions. This is a first date. You know, we're going to do a project together. It's the first date. If I like you and you like me and we produce good results together, then the goal of this first date is for us to get hitched. The goal of this is for us to have, and then I painted in the picture of the kind of relationship that I wanted with him over the long haul. And I explained to him why that was important. And I said, if you will accept this, if this sounds good to you, then I'll move ahead and I'll do this first project. Are you there? I'm listening. Okay, I'm sorry. My phone keeps making a call. Yeah, you are clicking a little bit. But if everyone loves everybody, then we'll get married. Otherwise, then I'll just move on. And there are other freelance copywriters you can work with. They always accept the first date. Then the second step then is you meet with them to discuss this first project. But while you're there, you spend time talking about their entire sales cycle. You look at what they're doing in terms of generating new customers and how they're working with their existing customers. You look at ways that they can increase sales at every step. You talk about offer tests and headline tests that they could do on other people's packages where you won't even make money on them. But you're sitting there in a meeting room with these people and they'll tend to try out everything they're doing. And you'll say, you know, that headline might work better if you just change this and that. Or this might work better if you change this and that. Or show me what you're doing on the web and they show you what they're doing on the web and you go, oh my goodness. Look at this big glaring gap in what you're doing on the web here. If you just added a pop-up here or a landing page there or an additional sale here, it could be worth tens or hundreds of thousands more dollars to your company. In any copywriter, anybody who's done any of the, you know, what I've suggested so many times, learning the nuts and bolts of the business, but also just using your own common sense, can look at something that's being done and suggest improvements. Now what have you done? You just gave them, in a day, you gave them four or five ideas. Each one of them could be worth a hundred thousand dollars or a million dollars. Right. And you've done it in a matter of minutes, right? And it's just kind of casual off the top of your head. You're not selling anything. Well, usually at the end of those meetings, all they want to do is deepen the relationship. Because they start thinking, oh my goodness, you know, what if we did this once a month, you know, or once a quarter with the writer? And sat down with him and really looked at all of these other pieces. You know, it only takes him a little bit of his time and he's strengthening packages. Other writers have written for us, given us new test sell ideas. I had a situation the other day where I came into a very excellent company with top notch employees and I found a glaring gap in their internet strategy. And I just looked at it and I said, wait, wait, wait, what happens after that? You know, after you sell the product, the main product. And I said, well, we fulfill it and then later on we send them a promotion, you know, trying to sell them our back end product. And I said, look, the guy is sitting there at his computer screen in his underwear with his credit card in his hand. He has just bought your first product. And you're just letting him get away with that? You know, the next thing that needs to happen right after he fills out the shopping cart and buys that product is that you need to give him another landing page for your upsell product. Whoa. I mean, it looked like, you know, this 15 of some of the smartest people I've ever seen, but just because I was looking at it with fresh eyes, I was able to see something that they had and that's going to be worth, my guess is, tens of millions of dollars to them over the next couple of years. Now, do you let the client think you're really smart or do you tell him what you just said, which is, hey, look, you guys are smart. I'm just looking at this with fresh eyes. Well, I don't, you know, everyone has their own style. Mine is to go in as a very humble person and just say, look, guys, you know, whenever I see something where I can make a suggestion, I say, have you tried this? Mm-hmm. You know, and if they sometimes they'll say, yeah, we tried it, it doesn't work. So if you say, have you tried this, then you're protected it. They say, yeah, we tested that. It didn't work, right? So you don't look like an idiot. Right. You don't go in there and pronounce it. If you do this, blah, blah, blah. Yeah. Now, I know another guy who's everybody would know his name, who will walk in and he'll say, do this and it'll double your sales. You know, and the last time he tried that, he got his head handed to him. He looked like a fool. You know, there are no absolutes in this business. It's all creative. It's all try this, then try that kind of thing, right? And instinctive. And so, you know, recognize that you don't know it all and just, you know, be humble in your suggestions. The other thing is, because you don't know the history of the company and when you walk in, you don't know all the details. You don't know what the political situation is. You don't know what the legal beagles have set for them as far as restrictions or limitations, you know. It's just better to go in. This is fact-finding. This is just I want to get to know you guys better and I want to get to know the process better. Now, let's say you have that meeting and they're interested in having a more exclusive relationship with you. What are the rules of engagement? Not the money, but in other words, you tell them, I'll only work on you. I'll come into your office three days a week. What are the rules of the relationship? What's worked best for me is, well, you know, first of all, that basically once they see the value there, you don't have to sell them on a deeper relationship. At that point, they're trying to sell you on a deeper relationship because they see the value. You didn't have to sell them and tell them, well, I'll do this or I'll do that. You just go in and you demonstrate. So, like somebody at a client the other day that basically, I feel like a heroin dealer. I've just come in and I've given you free samples, you know, but now you're hooked. And I know that we're going to be partners now for a very long time. And it was only after they had come to mute that, oh, we want more of this, right? So, the format that has worked best for me is that I tend to like to go to physically visit my partners once a quarter and have usually two to three days of meetings. So, I'll generally spend a half a day on each product that I'm working on, on each franchise or product line that I'm working on. We'll take out everything that they've mailed. We'll look at everything their competitors are mailing. We'll look at their in-house promotions and their web promotions. And we will brainstorm what we want to do next. I tell them, when I come in there, what I need you to tell me is what do your prospects fear the most? What frustrates them the most and what do they desire most? You tell me those three things. If you're right, I'll give you a 4% response rate, okay? If you're right on two of them, we won't do quite as well, but we'll still do great. You even get one of them right and we'll have a good, strong control. And let's go over those three things again. It's what frustrates them. It's what they fear the most. What frustrates them the most and what they desire the most. You tell me that and then I can give you strong controls. And the more right you are about your answers to those three questions, the stronger our mailings are going to be. And so in preparations for those meetings, the clients are doing several things. They're trying to answer those three questions and they can do that through surveys. They can do that through just general market research. They can do that by diving into the prospect files and finding out what those people have responded to in the past. And so I go in there and there's no pressure on me. My job is to learn. They have excellent marketing departments. They've done their assignment, which is to prepare for the meeting by giving us a state of the market. Where are our prospects? What is it that's driving them right? What are the answers to those three questions? I listen to that. Then I look at what we've tried. We do a post-mortem on what we've tried, what worked, what didn't work, what we think could have worked better. We look at what competitors are doing. And we can always tell how well competitors are doing because we know how many times they're coming back and running our list for each package. And we know how many times we see a particular package in the mail or on the list where we've seeded our own names. So we do our competitive survey. And then we sit around and we brainstorm what we're going to do in the next quarter for each one of the products that the customer has. What are we going to do to the client has? What are we going to do to bring in more customers? What are we going to do to sell more things to those customers? And what are we going to do on the web with these people? And so at the end of that two or three day period, depending on how many products we have that we have to deal with, we will have... And I like to record these sessions and get a transcript. But at the end of that period of time, we will have a strategy for the next 90 days for copy for each one of his projects, each one of his products. Then I go home and I write up a platform and a research request for each one of those projects. And it won't have the headline. It'll just say we're going to present this product in this way. Here's the major theme of the promotion. Here's what we're going to say about that major theme. And here's the research that I need your people to provide or that somebody is going to have to provide. In most cases, I turn to my client to provide that research in order to write these promotions. The client then takes that and we call it a platform. The client then takes that platform and research request, circulates it to whoever in the organization should have input into that, and then sends it back to me marked up with their comments. I don't respond to that. I then take...in other words, I don't go back and say, well, here's your second draft platform. I just say, okay, that's good. That's a done deal. Then I either...if I'm going to write it myself, I wait until the research package comes in, or if I have to produce it myself, I either do it myself or I hire someone to do it for me. And then we go to work. And we write the package, we write the promotion, and we submit it to the client, and we go through the normal process. And then after the quarter's over, we go back for our next meeting, and we lay out the next quarter. So that's the working methodology. Now, there's two questions. We have 10 minutes, and I think we've got to get these in because people want to know it. Number one, they want to know if this can happen to me. What are the...we don't need to know what you get paid exactly, but you've been very forthcoming in these series about fees. What should someone be looking to charge here for giving a client this kind of service and relationship? I tend to have a standing deal that was kind of born out of my direct mail royalty. My direct mail royalty runs anywhere from 8% to 10% of what it costs them to mail a customer acquisition package to the world. Okay? So they're paying $500 a thousand. They were...you know, sometimes it's $560, sometimes it's $480, but I figure about $500 a thousand to mail a promotion package that's for postage, printing, mailing list, and everything. And on those acquisition packages, I consistently receive between $30 and $50 per thousand pieces mail. So they're paying other people $500 a thousand to mail this package, and then they're paying me a $50 a thousand royalty. So I'm being paid 10% of their promotion cost basically in terms of a royalty. Right. And those promotions are designed to break even. So I'm also being paid 10% of the revenues that I'm generating on that promotion. You follow? I'm not sure I do. You're being paid...let's say the mailing cost is $500 per thousand. You automatically get, regardless of the revenue, $50 for that. 10% of the mailing cost. So you're going into the payment. You get paid based on the cost of the marketing, the cost of the promotion, but you also get paid based on the revenue generated? Right. Well, what I do is, because on acquisition packages, I'm being paid roughly 10% of revenues. Right. Because they're mailing to break even, right? And break even is $500 a thousand because that's what the cost is. I simply adapt that across the board, and I say, okay, when I'm doing a promotion, when I'm doing a promotion for you that's designed to make you a profit, I also want to... Aha, 10%. Okay. Yes. I think it's imminently fair. Now, can you get more? Absolutely. I know a copywriter that gets 30% of revenues. But now we're talking just basically negotiation, and it quite often depends on who the client is. And frankly, I think 10% is very, very fair. And so far, everyone I've talked to thinks it's fair, too. And then, so if the proof... Let's say I write one piece of copy and they decide to send it out to generate new customers. So they're doing it at break even, so there is no profit, and so I give my $50 a thousand or $30 a thousand. But if they mail that same copy internally to existing customers, they're going to make a profit because their response rate is going to be enormous. Right. You get 10% of that profit. And I'll get 10% of that. If they then decide to use that copy on the web, not in a direct mail piece, but on the web, again, 10% of revenues. And it just...it requires them to do some accounting changes so that they can track this stuff. But it's well worth it because what you're bringing to the table is huge in terms of additional revenue for the client. Now, the script for getting into this you sort of gave us already. If I want to do this, I want to basically say when someone asks me about doing a package, I want to say what you said. I want to say, well, I could do that, but what interests me more is, and then suggest this, and go into your analogy or metaphor about, you know, this being the engagement and that we want to get hitched, and then try to drive for that first meeting where I can give them what you called the free samples of what my advice can do for them. Right. That's right. And let me just add a couple of things here, okay? The deal that you structure with a client will depend to a great degree upon the client how they're structured, what their experience with direct response is, the value that they place on copy, and all of those other things. But there are a lot of ways to get paid in this kind of relationship. As I mentioned earlier, there was one client where I said, look, what I'm doing here is, yes, I'm providing all this copy for you, but as you'll notice when I come in every quarter, I'm also giving you ideas that you're using on product lines that I'm not promoting. Right. And I'm giving you general marketing ideas that are worth a lot of money to your company, and I'm not being paid for that. And I think it's only fair that I do get paid for that. And so quite often I will add a retainer that can be back in the 90s with Phillips. It was $10,000 a month. These days it's $25,000 a month that basically compensates me for that stuff that's not trackable. But I'm coming in and I'm providing tremendous value to the company. I should be compensated for that. And so a monthly retainer is also possible in these situations. A few years ago I came into a company that wanted, and this is important too for, I think, for copywriters to understand, every dollar that the client pays you upfront costs him money and costs you some money too down the road, because that's a dollar that he's not able to invest in mailing or distributing your sales copy, and therefore that's more customers he isn't going to get because he's paying you instead of paying postage and other things. So in some cases when very aggressive growth is the goal and when the customer is fully on board with that to the point where he'll mail it a loss or he'll do whatever is necessary, he's really investing in his company now, quite often a way to go is to say, instead of charging you a monthly retainer for these extra services, I want stock options. That way you're not paying me anything today, so you can use 100% of the capital you have available to you to grow. But at some point in the future, say, set a time limit three years from now, I want to be able to call you up and say, I'm exercising my options for 10% of the company's value three years from now, or whatever percent the two of you are able to negotiate. And if you want to really make it easy for the guy to say yes, you can say it'll be 10% or 20% of the increase. So the stock option is structured in such a way that the current value of the company is established using a formula that, you know, his accountant will help him work out their fairly standard formulas. And the company's value is established today at, say, $10 million. Three years from now, you go to exercise your option, and the company is worth $30 million. Three years from now, then you say, okay, I'm exercising my option, and they will say, okay, the difference in the company value is $20 million, and the contract provides that you will receive some percent of that increase, whether it's 10% or 20%. And I've done this. This is not a hypothetical. It does work, and it has to be drawn up in a contract because that's bulletproof. So one thing you have to do is be sure to give your own attorney involved in this process. If you do that, and it's important, even if you totally trust this client, love this client, you know, know he'd take a bullet for you. The fact of the matter is he could get hit by a bus. And the person who takes over says, I don't know what you're talking about. Right. And so it needs to be drawn out in great detail. You also need to have a clause in there that says that should you get hit by the bus, that your estate will still receive the compensation. Yes, because some of us have estates that actually want us to be hit by buses, so we have to be careful. But I want to underscore the importance of this. Now, think about, now put yourself into the client's shoes, right? And you're a copywriter. You've had your first date. You've come in, and you've given him a winner, right? Then you've sat down with his people, and you've spun out ideas that bring money to his bottom line, where he can see, and Paris did this, by the way. I think it was with Chip Wood, where he just showed up at the guy's office, sat down with him, and went through all of his newsletter inserts. Now, this stuff, a copywriter, has never seen or touched before. But he sat down with the inserts, the guy puts it in each month's issue of his newsletters, and said, you know what? You could really make a lot more money with these inserts if you would change them in these ways. And that ended up in a long-term relationship between Paris and that client. And I'm not privy to the deal structure, although Paris called me before he structured the deal to see what my advice was on it, but I don't know how it came out. But I know that it's very profitable for both of them. It's made both of them. I know. Now, on that note, we're out of time for today, so what we're going to do next week, and folks, we'll be back for our last, fifth, and final call on Clayton's Coffee Riding Secrets Wednesday at noon. We're going to plan on an hour and a half from 12 to 1.30. What we'll do is we have some questions from Hans and David and others that we'll answer. Clayton's going to give us a lot of coffee writing secrets. And also, I just want to say, I've only been working with Clayton a short time. And before that, I've been writing coffee for 25 years, and I've already changed based on working with him the way I write coffee. I will give you at the beginning of the call what I think are the two coffee writing techniques he uses or teaches that have made an incredible difference in what I do. And that I now use in every promotion, and I've only started doing so recently. So again, I want to thank you for listening today and going the extra half hour, and we'll see you guys at noon. Thanks, Clayton. Thank you, guys. Thanks, Julie. See you next week. Bye-bye.
